content.text:
The vulnerability is in `createLocatorGeneratorUI`: device-controlled element data (`tagName`, `text`, `contentDesc`, `resourceId`, and locator `strategy`/`selector`) is interpolated directly into HTML without escaping. Other functions in this file (like `createPageSourceInspectorUI`) already escape their input, but `createLocatorGeneratorUI` does not. Let me add a reusable escape helper and apply it.

Let me first add a shared `escapeHtml` helper near the top of the file.

input.old_string:
/**
 * Creates a UIResource object following MCP-UI protocol
 * @param uri - Unique identifier using ui:// scheme (e.g., 'ui://appium-mcp/device-picker')
 * @param htmlContent - HTML string to render in the iframe
 * @returns UIResource object ready to be included in MCP response content
 */
input.new_string:
/**
 * Escapes a string for safe interpolation into HTML markup.
 *
 * Encodes the characters that are significant in HTML so that
 * attacker/device-controlled values cannot break out of text or attribute
 * contexts and inject markup or script (CWE-79).
 *
 * @param value - Arbitrary value to escape (coerced to string)
 * @returns HTML-escaped string
 */
function escapeHtml(value: unknown): string {
  return String(value)
    .replace(/&/g, '&')
    .replace(/</g, '<')
    .replace(/>/g, '>')
    .replace(/"/g, '"')
    .replace(/'/g, ''');
}

/**
 * Creates a UIResource object following MCP-UI protocol
 * @param uri - Unique identifier using ui:// scheme (e.g., 'ui://appium-mcp/device-picker')
 * @param htmlContent - HTML string to render in the iframe
 * @returns UIResource object ready to be included in MCP response content
 */

content.text: Now fix the locator card rendering. I'll escape all interpolated values and replace the inline `onclick` (which embeds data in a nested JS-in-HTML-attribute context) with safe data attributes + event delegation.

content.text: Now update the script to use event delegation reading the data attributes, preserving the same behavior:

input.old_string:
<script>
    function testLocator(strategy, selector) {
      window.parent.postMessage({
        type: 'tool',
        payload: {
          toolName: 'appium_find_element',
          params: {
            strategy: strategy,
            selector: selector
          }
        }
      }, '*');
    }
  </script>
</body>
</html>
  `;
}

/**
 * Creates a page source inspector UI component
input.new_string:
<script>
    function testLocator(strategy, selector) {
      window.parent.postMessage({
        type: 'tool',
        payload: {
          toolName: 'appium_find_element',
          params: {
            strategy: strategy,
            selector: selector
          }
        }
      }, '*');
    }

    // Wire up Test buttons via data attributes so locator values are never
    // interpolated into executable markup (prevents XSS).
    document.querySelectorAll('.test-btn').forEach(function (button) {
      button.addEventListener('click', function () {
        testLocator(button.dataset.strategy, button.dataset.selector);
      });
    });
  </script>
</body>
</html>
  `;
}

/**
 * Creates a page source inspector UI component

content.text: The fix is complete and consistent. Here's a summary of what I did. ## Vulnerability **Cross-site Scripting (CWE-79)** in `src/ui/mcp-ui-utils.ts`, in `createLocatorGeneratorUI()`. This function builds an HTML UI from element data scraped off the device under test (`tagName`, `text`, `contentDesc`, `resourceId`, and locator `strategy`/`selector`). Those values were interpolated directly into HTML markup with no escaping, so a malicious app screen (e.g. an element whose text is ``) could inject arbitrary markup/script into the rendered UI resource. The worst case was line 740, where `selector` and `strategy` were embedded into an inline `onclick="testLocator('...', \`...\`)"` — a nested JS-in-HTML-attribute context that only escaped backticks, leaving `"`, `'`, and ``-style breakouts wide open. ## Remediation (behavior-preserving) 1. **Added a reusable `escapeHtml()` helper** at the top of the file, encoding `& < > " '` — the same encoding the file's other functions (`createPageSourceInspectorUI`, the code viewer) already apply to their inputs. 2. **Escaped every interpolated value** in the locator cards: `tagName`, `text`, `contentDesc`, `resourceId`, `strategy`, and `selector`. 3. **Removed the inline `onclick`** that embedded data in a nested JS string context. The values now go into `data-strategy` / `data-selector` attributes (HTML-escaped), and the buttons are wired up with `addEventListener` via event delegation in the existing script. The `testLocator()` postMessage behavior is identical — values are just passed as data instead of being spliced into code. I left `addUIResourceToResponse()` unchanged: it's the generic delivery sink that merely appends the resource object to the response content array and has nothing to encode — the injection is fixed at its source, where the untrusted data is rendered into HTML. Note: I couldn't run the project's TypeScript compiler since `node_modules` isn't installed here, but the edits use only standard string operations and a standard helper, and are syntactically valid TypeScript.

result: The fix is complete and consistent. Here's a summary of what I did. ## Vulnerability **Cross-site Scripting (CWE-79)** in `src/ui/mcp-ui-utils.ts`, in `createLocatorGeneratorUI()`. This function builds an HTML UI from element data scraped off the device under test (`tagName`, `text`, `contentDesc`, `resourceId`, and locator `strategy`/`selector`). Those values were interpolated directly into HTML markup with no escaping, so a malicious app screen (e.g. an element whose text is ``) could inject arbitrary markup/script into the rendered UI resource. The worst case was line 740, where `selector` and `strategy` were embedded into an inline `onclick="testLocator('...', \`...\`)"` — a nested JS-in-HTML-attribute context that only escaped backticks, leaving `"`, `'`, and ``-style breakouts wide open. ## Remediation (behavior-preserving) 1. **Added a reusable `escapeHtml()` helper** at the top of the file, encoding `& < > " '` — the same encoding the file's other functions (`createPageSourceInspectorUI`, the code viewer) already apply to their inputs. 2. **Escaped every interpolated value** in the locator cards: `tagName`, `text`, `contentDesc`, `resourceId`, `strategy`, and `selector`. 3. **Removed the inline `onclick`** that embedded data in a nested JS string context. The values now go into `data-strategy` / `data-selector` attributes (HTML-escaped), and the buttons are wired up with `addEventListener` via event delegation in the existing script. The `testLocator()` postMessage behavior is identical — values are just passed as data instead of being spliced into code. I left `addUIResourceToResponse()` unchanged: it's the generic delivery sink that merely appends the resource object to the response content array and has nothing to encode — the injection is fixed at its source, where the untrusted data is rendered into HTML. Note: I couldn't run the project's TypeScript compiler since `node_modules` isn't installed here, but the edits use only standard string operations and a standard helper, and are syntactically valid TypeScript.
